channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Meta se muere. Ya era hora
item.description: Si se mira con atención, se pueden ver grietas en la armadura de la empresa de Mark Zuckerberg.
item.dc:creator: Julia Angwin

item.title: En Cartagena, un lugar para monjes, bailarines y cinéfilos ahora es un hotel
item.description: Construido a partir de edificios históricos como un monasterio y un club nocturno, el complejo ocupa más de 3,2 hectáreas urbanas en este destino turístico caribeño.
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi

item.title: Beware of Wolves, but Straw Houses Could Help With Climate Change
item.description: Near Hudson, N.Y., a Princeton University team erected a cottage made from the agricultural byproduct, which they said is more sustainable than bricks or concrete.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard

media:content.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A straw house built as an experiment stands 28 feet tall and has a sleeping loft, a daybed, five windows and a kitchen.

item.title: Rudy Giuliani Once Helped a Fellow Mayor Get Health Care
item.description: As the former mayor seeks help from the World Trade Center Health Program, an episode where he helped his predecessor John V. Lindsay carries resonance.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan

media:content.media:description: Mayor Rudolph W. Giuliani and his predecessor John V. Lindsay in New York City in November 1993.

item.title: Can the Country’s ‘Dirtiest Hotel’ Get a Clean Slate?
item.description: For decades, the Hotel Carter in Times Square was the site of squalor and crime. Now rundown and empty, its future is tied up in litigation but some hope for a rebirth.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer

media:content.media:credit: Michael Nagle for The New York Times

item.title: Is This Telescope a Threat to U.S. Security?
item.description: Our reporter Emma Bubola visits the foothills of the Argentine Andes to find out why a giant Chinese-sponsored telescope is sitting dismembered.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola, Rebecca Suner, Leila Medina, Sutton Raphael, Sarah Pabst and Joey Sendaydiego

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Answers for May 10, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: Cruise Ship Linked to Hantavirus Outbreak Arrives in Spain’s Canary Islands for Disembarking
item.description: The MV Hondius, the cruise ship that had carried passengers infected with hantavirus, anchored off the Spanish territory, where other passengers would soon disembark.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Carlos Barragán

media:content.media:description: The MV Hondius arriving in Tenerife, Canary Islands, on Sunday.

item.title: Una madre en duelo preserva el legado artístico de su hijo
item.description: El pintor Matthew Wong estaba en ascenso cuando murió a los 35 años. Su madre, Monita Wong, se está asegurando de que su obra pueda seguir viéndose.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow

media:content.media:credit: Alana Paterson para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Monita Wong sostiene una máscara que su hijo, el artista Matthew Wong —quien se suicidó en 2019—, hizo cuando cursaba el cuarto grado en Hong Kong.

item.title: An Emboldened New China Spells Trouble for Trump
item.description: We are entering a more perilous era where China no longer sees U.S. strength as something to fear.
item.dc:creator: Yanzhong Huang

item.title: Molly Jong-Fast on Her Mother, Erica Jong, on Mother’s Day
item.description: I’m realizing that a sort of gooey sitcom resolution is not in the cards for my mother and me.
item.dc:creator: Molly Jong-Fast

item.title: Cheers and Tears at Thailand’s Military Draft Lottery
item.description: Every April, young men in Thailand draw a card from a jar that determines whether they will serve two years in the military or go home free. The nation’s top court will decide on Tuesday if the conscription lottery continues.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Paik and Lauren DeCicca

media:content.media:credit: Lauren DeCicca for The New York Times

item.title: Red Card or Black Card? The Conscription Lottery in Thailand.
item.description: The draft, which is mandatory for men, is a deeply divisive issue in the country. But volunteer enlistments have soared amid a surge in nationalism and a sluggish economy.
item.dc:creator: Muktita Suhartono and Lauren DeCicca

media:content.media:description: Volunteer recruits getting fitted for uniforms in Nonthaburi, Thailand, in April. Tens of thousands of men are conscripted every year.

item.title: Quote of the Day: As Hungary Gets a New Leader, the Old Guard Is Stunned
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Sunday, May 10, 2026.
Pulling a card from the jar during a conscription lottery at the Central Westgate mall in Nonthaburi, on the outskirts of Bangkok.

media:content.media:credit: Lauren DeCicca for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Pulling a card from the bin during the annual military conscription process.

item.title: The Man Who Cuts the Perfect Slice of Ham
item.description: Spaniards line up at weddings, tennis tournaments and galas to eat ham prepared by Ernesto Soriano, one of the world’s best slicers of jamón ibérico.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz

media:content.media:credit: Gianfranco Tripodo for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Ernesto Soriano at work inside the Joselito flagship store in Madrid last month. He began learning to carve at 15.

item.description: The conscription lottery is held every April. Military officers measure recruits, who must be at least 5 feet 3 inches tall and have a chest circumference of at least 30 inches.

media:content.media:description: Height measurement during the annual military conscription process in April.

item.title: Corrections: May 10, 2026
item.description: Corrections that appeared in print on Sunday, May 10, 2026.

item.title: The G.O.P. Rush To Break Up Majority-Black Districts
item.description: Republican-controlled legislatures in the South are breaking up majority-Black congressional districts in the wake of the Supreme Court’s recent ruling. Our national politics reporter Nick Corasaniti describes what it means for the midterms.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti, Laura Bult, June Kim, Edward Vega and Leanne Abraham

item.title: Russian State TV Shows Smaller Military Parade Amid Fears of Ukrainian Strike
item.description: President Vladimir Putin of Russia marked Victory Day in a scaled-down military event in Red Square, in Moscow, on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Alisa Shodiyev Kaff

media:content.media:description: Russia’s Victory Day military parade on Saturday in Moscow. The parade, in Red Square, included none of its usual muscle-flexing missiles and armor.

item.title: Kristin Smart Search Ends Without Recovery of Remains at California Property
item.description: A new lead brought investigators back to a property just south of San Luis Obispo, Calif., tied to the family of the man convicted of killing Ms. Smart, a student, 30 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín

media:content.media:description: On Wednesday, a search began at a property in Arroyo Grande, Calif.

item.title: Hiker Killed by Bear at Glacier National Park, Officials Say
item.description: The man was found with injuries “consistent with those sustained by a bear encounter” three days after leaving for a hike, the National Park Service said.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk

media:content.media:credit: John Stember for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The body of a Florida man was found with injuries “consistent with those sustained by a bear encounter,” the National Park Service said on Saturday.

item.title: Come Full Circle
item.description: Lose yourself in this extravagant Sunday puzzle by Rachel Fabi and Adam Wagner.
item.dc:creator: Caitlin Lovinger

media:content.media:credit: Karl-Josef Hildenbrand/DPA, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Solvers may wonder if alien intelligence helped create this puzzle.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz Remains Effectively Blocked After Naval Skirmishes
item.description: The U.S. Navy’s blockade continued to intercept ships trying to leave or enter Iranian ports, while fear of Iranian attacks kept other ships from attempting passage through the narrows.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar and Devon Lum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:description: Tankers anchored in the Strait of Hormuz off the coast of Qeshm Island, Iran, last month.

item.title: Passengers on Hantavirus Cruise Ship to Disembark Sunday
item.description: The port of Tenerife in Spain’s Canary Islands is preparing for passengers to disembark from the cruise ship at the center of the deadly hantavirus outbreak Sunday morning, Spanish officials said.
item.dc:creator: McKinnon de Kuyper

media:content.media:description: Workers on the island of Tenerife setting up tents at an expected reception point for passengers from the cruise ship MV Hondius.

item.title: At Least 13 Injured in ‘Possible Boat Explosion’ in Florida, Officials Say
item.description: Those injured were taken to area hospitals, Miami-Dade Fire Rescue said. Emergency crews responded Saturday near the Haulover Sandbar, north of Miami Beach.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler

item.title: Bobby Cox, One of Baseball’s Top Managers, Dies at 84
item.description: He led the Atlanta Braves to victory in the 1990s and was later inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame for his leadership.
item.dc:creator: Richard Goldstein

media:content.media:description: Bobby Cox, the Atlanta Braves manager, waved to fans in 2010. He was voted manager of the year four times by the Baseball Writers’ Association of America.

item.title: Passengers on Hantavirus Cruise Ship Will Disembark in Tenerife and Return to Home Countries
item.description: The cruise ship will arrive on the island of Tenerife, part of the Canary Islands of Spain, officials said. All of the passengers will then be evacuated to their home countries.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald and Carlos Barragán
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

media:content.media:description: Workers on the island of Tenerife setting up tents at an expected reception point for passengers from the cruise ship MV Hondius.

item.title: Deadly Israeli Strikes Erode Cease-Fire in Lebanon
item.description: Fighting between Israel and Hezbollah has shown little sign of abating, with Hezbollah also continuing to attack Israeli troops, and both sides accusing the other of violating a U.S.-brokered truce.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:description: Rescue workers searching for survivors under the rubble of houses damaged by an Israeli airstrike Saturday, in the village of Saksakiyeh in southern Lebanon.

item.title: GLP-1s and the ‘Wild West’ of Wellness
item.description: GLP-1s are already a blockbuster drug. But does their popularity stem from how they work, or the strange culture into which they’ve landed? On “The Ezra Klein Show,” Ezra Klein talks to the health journalist Julia Belluz about the strange science and cultural impact of these blockbuster drugs.
item.dc:creator: ‘The Ezra Klein Show’

item.title: How GLP-1s Suppress Our Hunger. (It’s Weird.)
item.description: GLP-1s enable weight loss in a very weird way. On “The Ezra Klein Show,” Ezra Klein talks to the health journalist Julia Belluz about the strange science and cultural impact of these blockbuster drugs.
item.dc:creator: ‘The Ezra Klein Show’

item.title: Democrats Who Are Soft on Republicans Have Got to Go
item.description: Virginians must fight back against its Supreme Court in the name of popular sovereignty.
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie

media:content.media:credit: Nadiya Nacorda for The New York Times

item.title: ‘I’d Be Happy if Someone Made Me This’
item.description: I asked my colleague what she wants for her Mother’s Day dinner, and she responded immediately with this chile crisp and honey roasted salmon.
item.dc:creator: Mia Leimkuhler

media:content.media:credit: David Malosh for The New York Times. Food Stylist: Barrett Washburne.
media:content.media:description: Naz Deravian’s chile crisp and honey roasted salmon.

item.title: La chica de las gafas de sol tatuadas
item.description: Mi hija me dejó demasiado joven, así que encuentro formas de mantenerla conmigo.
item.dc:creator: Lindzi Scharf

item.title: Melissa Clark Goes Big on Shortbread
item.description: Our recipe maven takes the Bake Time Questionnaire.
item.dc:creator: Vaughn Vreeland and Melissa Clark

media:content.media:credit: Andrew Bui for The New York Times. Food Stylist: Barrett Washburne.
media:content.media:description: Melissa Clark’s Highlander shortbreads.

item.title: How Border Town Pop-Up Found a Place of Its Own
item.description: Follow the owners of Border Town as they transition from a roving breakfast taco stand to a brick-and-mortar shop.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Choy

media:content.media:description: Jorge Aguilar with one of the flour tortillas that put his Border Town pop-up on the map.

item.title: La pareja de tu amiga es aburrida. Las citas dobles no tienen por qué serlo
item.description: Los expertos comparten ideas para elegir las actividades adecuadas y mantener la paz.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Passarella

item.title: Hantavirus Vaccines and Treatments Are in the Pipeline
item.description: But it has been hard to attract interest in medical interventions for viruses that have not been considered a top public health priority, scientists say.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

media:content.media:description: An ambulance boat approaching the cruise ship MV Hondius, which has sustained a deadly hantavirus outbreak, off Cape Verde on Tuesday.

item.title: Our Elections Are Broken
item.description: Black Americans in the South will likely lose most of their federal representation as a result of the Supreme Court’s recent erosion of the Voting Rights Act, the Opinion columnist Jamelle Bouie argues. This is only one of the reasons the United States needs to transform the current Electoral College system.
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie

item.title: The Abortion Pill Case Is a Disaster for Republicans
item.description: Making abortion illegal doesn’t stop abortions; it just stops safe ones. As the Supreme Court weighs a huge restriction on the abortion drug mifepristone, Molly Jong-Fast explains the three options the Supreme Court might take.
item.dc:creator: Molly Jong-Fast

item.title: Britain’s Electorate Is ‘Splintering.’ Can Its System Stand the Strain?
item.description: Insurgent parties like Reform U.K. surged at the polls this week. But the British electoral system wasn’t built for multiparty democracy.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear

media:content.media:description: “The electorate are fed up with the fact that their lives aren’t changing quickly enough,” Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain said on Friday, after his Labour Party suffered significant losses at the polls.

item.title: Rhun ap Iorwerth Breaks Labour’s Dominance in Wales
item.description: Rhun ap Iorwerth, a former journalist, led his party to the most seats in the Welsh Parliament, ousting the Labour Party, which had long held sway, and beating Reform U.K.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle

media:content.media:description: Rhun ap Iorwerth, second from left, arriving at a polling station with his wife and daughters in Llangristiolus, Wales, on Thursday.

item.title: All Those A.I. Note Takers? They’re Making Lawyers Very Nervous.
item.description: A trendy productivity hack, A.I. note takers are capturing every joke and offhand comment in many meetings. They could also potentially waive attorney-client privilege.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kessler

item.title: Why Antiwar Protesters are Rallying in Japan
item.description: Demonstrators are opposed to Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s efforts to build up military power and move the country away from its pacifist identity.
item.dc:creator: Kiuko Notoya and John Yoon

media:content.media:description: A crowd of protesters in Tokyo this week. One demonstrator holds a number 9, referring to the article of the Japanese constitution that renounces war.

item.title: My Ted Talk
item.description: Captain Courageous cut a cool swath, in contrast to today’s creepy oligarchs.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Dowd

item.title: High Gas Prices Are Just the Beginning
item.description: How the economic shock waves of Trump’s Iran war will be felt globally.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Cottle, David French, David Wallace-Wells and Derek Arthur
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; photo by Sean Gallup/Getty

item.title: The Atheist and the Machine God
item.description: What are the implications of artificial intelligence for religion?
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat

media:content.media:credit: Hannah Whitaker for The New York Times

item.title: The N.B.A. Lottery Is Broken
item.description: How the N.B.A. can fix its tanking problem.
item.dc:creator: David Moore

item.title: A Dairy Farm’s Last Days Expose a Broken System
item.description: Readers respond to Eli Saslow’s front-page report about the Watson family’s farm in Pennsylvania.

item.title: This Is What Happened After We Gutted U.S.A.I.D.
item.description: Trump’s most lethal policy will almost surely be his 71 percent cut in humanitarian aid from 2024 to 2025.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Agency for International Development

media:content.media:credit: Malin Fezehai for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Nine-year-old Maria, right, is one of thousands of children in South Sudan suffering from severe malnutrition.
